data.title: Employment Report: 73K Jobs Added In July, Lower Than Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The latest employment report showed that 73,000 jobs were added in July, up from 14,000 in June but lower than the expected 106,000 addition. Meanwhile, the unemployment rate inched up to 4.2%, as expected.

Here is an excerpt from the Employment Situation Summary released this morning by the Bureau of Labor Statistics:

Total nonfarm payroll employment changed little in July (+73,000) and has shown little change since April, the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) reported today. The unemployment rate, at 4.2 percent, also changed little in July. Employment continued to trend up in health care and in social assistance. Federal government continued to lose jobs.

Household Survey Data: Both the unemployment rate, at 4.2 percent, and the number of unemployed people, at 7.2 million, changed little in July. The unemployment rate has remained in a narrow range of 4.0 percent to 4.2 percent since May 2024.

Establishment Survey Data: Total nonfarm payroll employment changed little in July (+73,000) and has shown little change since April. Over the month, employment continued to trend up in health care and in social assistance. Federal government continued to lose jobs.

Here is a snapshot of the monthly change in nonfarm employment over the last four years. The 3-month moving average is currently at 35,000, its lowest level since the initial aftermath of the pandemic.

For another view, here is the monthly percent change in nonfarm employment since 2000. We've added a 12-month moving average to highlight the long-term trend. The latest 12-month moving average is at 128,000, its lowest level since the initial aftermath of the pandemic.

Unemployment, Recessions, and Market Trends

The next chart illustrates the relationship between unemployment, recessions, and the S&P Composite since 1948. Unemployment is typically a lagging indicator that moves inversely to equity prices (the top series in the chart).

Notice the rising unemployment peaks in 1971, 1975, and 1982, which coincided with bear markets. A similar pattern briefly emerged during the COVID pandemic, but the impact was short-lived as irrational exuberance quickly took over.

The latest unemployment rate stands at 4.248% (to three decimal places), the highest level since October 2021.

Now, let's examine the unemployment rate as a recession indicator - specifically, the cyclical troughs in the unemployment rate (UR). The next chart highlights a 12-month moving average of the UR, with its troughs marked.

Currently, the unemployment rate stands at 4.25%, higher than the latest 12-month moving average of 4.15%. As shown in the inset table, the correlation between these moving average troughs and the start of recessions is remarkably strong. The most recent trough occurred 22 months ago, in June 2023, when the 12-month moving average of the unemployment rate fell to its lowest level since January 1970.

The next chart highlights the unemployment rate for those unemployed for 27 weeks or more. This rate has declined significantly from its all-time peak of 4.4% in April 2010. Following the COVID pandemic, it rose as high as 2.6% but has since trended downward. It now stands at 1.1%, the highest level since December 2021.

How long does unemployment typically last? As the next chart shows, the latest data indicates that the average duration of unemployment is 24.1 weeks, the highest level since April 2022. Historically, this metric tends to rise during and after recessions.

The Bureau of Labor Statistics’ broadest measure of unemployment, the U6 series, captures not only the unemployed but also the underemployed, marginally attached workers, and those who have stopped looking for work.

Often referred to as the 'real' unemployment rate, many economists consider it the most comprehensive gauge of labor market conditions. In contrast, the more widely cited unemployment rate only includes those who are unemployed and actively job-seeking within the past four weeks.

The U6 series currently stands at 7.9%, up from the previous month.

Labor Market Participation and Structural Shifts

For a broader perspective on the structural shifts in the U.S. labor market, the next chart illustrates the labor force participation rate. This metric measures the percentage of the civilian population aged 16 and over who are either employed or actively seeking work.

It provides a key estimate of an economy’s active workforce and serves as an important indicator of labor market health. Currently, the rate stands at 62.2%, the lowest level since November 2022.

Here’s another chart that provides a different perspective on the unemployment rate - unemployment divided by the labor force participation rate. This metric currently stands at 6.8%, up from the previous month.

The next chart overlays the unemployment rate with the employment-population ratio, which measures the number of employed people as a percentage of the total civilian population aged 16 and over. The inverse correlation between the two series is clear.

The data also highlights the accelerating growth of women in the workforce and the rise of two-income households in the early 1980s. Before the COVID pandemic, the employment-population ratio fluctuated between 54.9% and 64.7%.

The lower end of this range dates back to the Eisenhower era - a time of predominantly one-income households, the Korean War, and the novelty of soft drinks being sold in cans. In April 2020, the global pandemic drove the employment-population ratio to a record low of 51.2%. Since then, it has been gradually recovering toward its pre-pandemic range, with the latest reading at 59.6%, the lowest level since December 2021.

Looking ahead, both the employment-population ratio and labor force participation rate will be key metrics to watch. The first wave of baby boomers continues to exert downward pressure on these figures. The oldest boomers became eligible for early retirement at the onset of the Great Recession, and their transition to full retirement age will continue through 2030.

Notes: The start date of 1948 in the charts above was determined by the earliest monthly employment data collected by the Bureau of Labor Statistics. The best source for the historical data is the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis.

The S&P Composite is a splice of the S&P 500, which started in 1957, with the S&P 90, which preceded it. ETFs associated with the S&P 500 include: iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO), and SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPLG).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Ford Set To Revamp Battery Electric Vehicle Strategy Amid Declining Profitability
data.description:
If Ford Motor's (NYSE:F) new battery-electric architecture indeed proves to be the modern-day rebirth of Ford’s 1908 iconic car, then the “Model T moment” quip by Jim Farley, Ford CEO, may prove to be prescient. The automaker could definitely use such a moment.

Ford has scheduled disclosure of details of the new BEV architecture and electrification strategy for Aug. 11.

And should Ford’s new BEV architecture blossom into a similar mass-market hit in the U.S. as the Model T was more than a century ago, it’s a good bet that the automaker’s share price will quickly jump higher than its current unexciting price of about $11.

For the time being, all is hyperbole as Ford seeks to regroup after an initial multi-year, $50 billion investment into battery electrification that has unraveled due to changes in U.S. governmental policy, as well as to hesitancy toward BEVs on the part of non-Tesla-owning American consumers.

Black ink fading

Ford’s profitability is declining. As a result of tariff headwinds, Ford in its Q2 financial report now anticipates a full-year EBIT of $6.5 billion to $7.5 billion, down from the $7 billion to $8.5 billion guidance issued in February. Cash flow and capex remain roughly the same.

Ford, in its Q2 earnings release:

Reported second-quarter revenue of $50.2 billion, a 5% increase from the same period a year ago, which outpaced wholesale growth. Net loss was $36 million, which primarily includes special charges related to a field service action and expenses related to a previously announced cancellation of an electric vehicle program; adjusted earnings before interest and taxes were $2.1 billion, including $0.8 billion of adverse net tariff-related impacts.

“To accelerate that progress over our multiyear business plan, we are shifting capital towards Pro, partly funded by reallocating the resources on future EV programs,” Farley told shareholders and analysts on Wednesday. Ford Pro is the division that services commercial customers of vans, trucks, and associated software, a promising and profitable growth division.

The automaker said CapEx will total about $9 billion this year, slightly greater than an earlier estimate of $8 billion to $9 billion.

Ford Pro, the automaker’s most profitable division, generated $2.3 billion in EBIT with a margin of 12.3% on $18.8 billion in revenue. Software and physical services contributed 17% of Ford Pro’s EBIT on a trailing 12-month basis. In the quarter, Ford Pro paid subscriptions grew 24% year-over-year to 757,000.

Ford Model e, the BEV division, reported a second-quarter EBIT loss of $1.3 billion, a $179 million greater loss than the same quarter a year ago. Tariffs and investments in new technology accelerated the deficit.

New formula

In a major change of direction, Ford is switching the emphasis of the battery chemistry used for its BEV models to LFP (Lithium Iron Phosphate) from Lithium Ion, which relies on nickel, cobalt, and manganese commonly used in the current generation of BEVs. While less energy-dense than the current generation, LFP is more affordable and therefore more suitable for mass market models.

The LFP tech is licensed from CATL, the China-based global leader in batteries for BEVs as well as other uses.

The second Trump administration is significantly rolling back fuel efficiency and clean air regulations from the Biden era, which will reduce or eliminate fines and eliminate the need for Ford and other automakers to buy clean-air credits from Tesla (TSLA) and other makers of BEVs.

Farley told shareholders this week that Ford’s commitment to buy CO2 credits has already been reduced by $1.5 billion.

“Further changes will balance standards and customer choice and have the potential to unlock a multibillion-dollar opportunity over the next two years,” Farley said, “Primarily in Ford Blue (the internal combustion vehicle division), which has carried a lot of the compliance burden.” Though he didn’t spell out details, Farley strongly implied that Ford is likely to increase the capacity and availability of gasoline engines like the V8, which had been expensive for consumers and limited in availability because federal rules, penalties, and guidelines made their production and sale too costly.

With the latest regulatory relaxation, the addition of more powerful gasoline engines for models like Ford’s top-selling F150 series of full-size pickup trucks could drive substantial profit to the bottom line.

Repair needed

At the same time, warranty and field service costs to cover defects and poor quality remain a vexing and worrisome drain on Ford’s finances. In 2024, Ford spent nearly $6B on warranty claims, an increase of 22% over 2023, and topped the previous record held in 2023 by a billion. In the second quarter of this year, Ford spent $570 million to cover fuel injector defects in Bronco Sport, Escape, and Kuga models. This one issue accounted for half of the special charge that resulted in Ford’s net loss for the period, though the company said warranty costs are down from last year.

Ford faces a variety of opportunities that could vastly improve its return, as well as negative factors that are out of its control—such as import tariffs—that could dent profits. Ford’s operations in the U.S., which represent the bulk of its assets, are in a position to benefit from the latest relaxation of clean air and fuel efficiency rules. At the same time, the company must salvage, rationalize, and make productive its projected $50 billion investment in BEVs.

An additional risk for the company is China: Ford’s EBIT in that country has been positive, although vehicle sales in unit terms dropped sharply last year and this year. Farley has been frank and candid about the threat the Chinese industry poses to all Western automakers, in its home market and abroad, including eventually in the U.S., due to superior technology, design, and cost efficiency.

The current price of Ford shares probably represents fair value and then some. For long-term investors, F must show its mettle against Chinese competitors. The overhang of BEV investments and unresolved quality problems, both of which hurt profitability, prevent raising F from a Hold. A Model T moment certainly would answer many of these concerns.

data.title: Stay On The Revenue Side Of AI
data.description:
Companies of all stripes are trying to glom onto AI excitement. Even companies that are only tangentially involved will have the term AI appear 52 times in their conference calls.

AI is here and it's powerful, but how can investors differentiate between the businesses that truly benefit and those that are just clinging on to a trend?

I propose two simple and easy-to-follow rules that allow an investor to participate without getting swept up in the mania:

  1. Stay on the revenue side of AI
  2. Stick to a reasonable valuation

Lots of companies talk about how they are investing in their AI capabilities. That's a cost-line item that hopefully will pan out someday. Or it could be another metaverse.

Recall that shortly after the pandemic, people correctly predicted that videoconferencing would be the future of business.

Zoom Communications (ZM) shot up hundreds of percent to around $600. But then it became clear that despite them having an excellent platform, monetization was always going to be difficult.

I think many of the AI products will suffer a similar fate. Many companies will build useful and perhaps even broadly adopted tools, but monetization and margins are questionable.

I’m not suggesting that companies won’t be successful in selling AI, but rather that it's a massive unknown. Many will fail, and a few will succeed.

A More Reliable Way to Invest in AI

Rather than trying to make something with AI and then sell it, other companies sell the foundational infrastructure that makes AI possible. I call this the revenue side of AI, because the revenues are already flowing,

I'm certainly not the first to notice this distinction, as the market has already bid up many or even most of the revenue-side stocks.

  • Chips
  • IPPs (independent power producers)
  • Nuclear energy producers

NVDA and its chip peers have dominated the market.

The independent power producers, Constellation (CEG), Vistra (VST), and Talen (TLN), shown below, are up 650% to 950%.

Anything in the nuclear supply chain is soaring on hopes that it's the reliable power to fuel AI.

GE Vernova (GEV) is perhaps the poster child for the energy infrastructure boom.

Each of these categories has had legitimate fundamental success.

The latest PJM auction shows significantly increased prices, which will further bolster the earnings of the independent power producers.

GEV’s margins have increased significantly, and there is speculation they will rise to the mid-teens.

I wrote about and bought GEV shortly after its spin-off. It was a great position for me, but I sold too early and now have a healthy dose of seller’s remorse.

GEV’s revenues have increased steadily along with the margins.

Revenue growth in tandem with margin expansion has a multiplicative impact on earnings. However, even strong earnings growth could not keep pace with the explosive market price.

GE Vernova now trades at 87X forward earnings. The rest of the really hot revenue-side AI stocks follow the same pattern—strong earnings growth that hasn’t kept pace with stock price.

This leaves them trading at lofty multiples:

  • CEG – 35X forward
  • VST – 28.6X forward
  • TLN – 58.1X forward
  • NVDA – 40.1X forward

These multiples may not seem that high compared to the S&P, but keep in mind that these stocks are in sectors that have historically been quite cyclical.

A cyclical stock should trade at its highest multiple at the trough of the cycle and its lowest multiple at the peak of the cycle. Thus, to justify multiples this high, one would have to believe that these companies are closer to the trough than the peak. Given the explosive earnings growth they have already experienced, I find that difficult to believe.

It's possible the AI wave is so powerful that these industries are no longer cyclical and have been launched into secular growth. That's the sort of conviction one would need for investment at today’s prices to be justified.

Instead, I think there are more reliable gains to be had in the pockets of the revenue side of AI that have not yet been bid to the moon.

Value Plays on the Revenue Side of AI

We consider regulated utilities to be the best mix of value and growth for participating in AI. They're not typically considered AI names, so allow me to elaborate on how they benefit by contrasting them with the IPPs discussed earlier.

The independent power producers participate in market prices of reliable extra electricity, so they directly and swiftly benefit from the spike in PJM auction prices.

The swiftness has made their earnings spike and, in my opinion, oversubscribed by stock market prices.

Regulated electric utilities experience a similar demand spike, but due to their heavily regulated nature, they're much slower to respond.

These next few years will be an interesting time frame in which there is a tremendous imbalance in energy markets. The demand for electricity ramped up rapidly, while the production of additional electricity is a process that takes up to five years. Permitting, regulating, and physically building new plants all take a long time.

During this window of imbalance, the IPPs and suppliers like GEV are reaping the majority of benefits. However, as time goes on and supply catches up to demand, it will be the regulated utilities that enjoy long-term higher earnings.

Incremental demand for electricity has manifested in massive pipelines for just about all of the regulated electric utilities. They're putting in place plans to substantially grow their production and transmission infrastructure base over the next decade. Most of their pipelines are between 50% and 200% of the market cap of each company.

As this infrastructure is built, the rate base of each utility increases, and its earnings increase proportionally with the rate base. Most of the utilities are now projecting long-term annual earnings growth in the 6%-9% range.

This might not sound like much, particularly as the S&P has recently been growing a bit more than that. However, there are two factors that I think are being overlooked by the market.

  1. 6%-9% growth is a lot more for companies that don’t really have negative growth periods
  2. Valuation

During growth phases, the S&P might grow at a double-digit pace, but it also has periods where it has substantial negative growth. Thus, a portion of its growth during the good times is just making up for previously lost growth.

Electric utilities are different because they have largely avoided recessions. Thus, each 6% to 9% growth they put up is against record high growth. The growth is fresh, incremental growth rather than a portion of it making up for previous losses.

So really, the S&P is only growing faster in nominal terms when looking at a period that happens to be during an upswing.

The 6%-9% annual growth rate utilities are positioned to enjoy over the next decade, in combination with their dividends, significantly outperforms the long-term returns of the S&P.

Valuation

As electric utilities have not really been considered part of AI despite the massive spike in electricity demand that results, market prices have remained low. The entire sector is trading at a significant discount to the broader market.

Between discounted valuation and high sustainable growth, I think regulated electric utilities will outperform the market.

data.title: Aeva Technologies: A Promising Vision Facing Harsh Realities
data.description:
Aeva Technologies (NASDAQ:AEVA) continues to seek a place among the most advanced LiDAR technology developers, but despite the visions of autonomous mobility and next-generation sensory platforms, investors still have many unanswered questions. The company’s Aeva Day event and news about cooperation with LG Innotek were certainly a step in the right direction, but I think their practical value to investors remains vague. At the same time, financial health reflects the same old reality: high losses, high operating costs, low revenue, and still far from profitability.

Financial Health and Q2 2025

AEVA Technologies reported a record quarter. The company earned $5.5 million in revenue. At first glance, it may seem like a strong step forward, and don’t get me wrong, it is a good step. However, the company’s financial position still isn’t safe. Although operating losses decreased slightly ($34.9 million), AEVA is still burning cash and remains far from profitability.

It is even more surprising that the overall loss totaled $192.7 million. Why so much? Not because of direct business losses. This is more related to accounting entries, for example, the revaluation of existing warrant liabilities and the share‑subscription liability tied to the LG Innotek investment. In other words, these are mostly paper losses, not direct cash losses. However, they show that the company’s reported profit or loss is highly sensitive to share price movements and other non-operational variables.

Another important factor is the cash situation. At the end of the quarter, AEVA held $49.8 million in cash and short-term investments. Does that sound like a lot? Not really. The company burned through approximately $60.6 million in the first half of the year, about $10 million per month. At this pace, cash could run out in 4-5 months. Yes, AEVA has a signed agreement to raise up to $125 million from investors, but this would require issuing new shares, which means existing shareholders could be diluted.

Another unpleasant fact is that AEVA's balance sheet shows negative equity: -$118 million. This means the company’s liabilities exceed its assets. Although mostly due to accounting adjustments, it’s still a serious warning signal that the financial position is weak. Negative equity is often seen in companies that struggle to stay viable over the long term.

Finally, although AEVA speaks about future technologies, advanced 4D LiDAR, and potential customers in the automotive sector, current revenue is mostly coming from small prototype orders, not large, recurring contracts. Without a stable cash flow or a visible path to profitability, this company still appears to be carrying a high-risk profile. Investors should be aware that AEVA may need to raise capital again, which could mean more shares, more dilution, and more uncertainty. But that’s my speculation.

Catalysts

AEVA is positioning itself as one of the technological leaders on the LiDAR market, but so far, I believe the development of the company is driven more by expectations than by evidence. However, there are currently some potential catalysts to distinguish, which, if realized, might help AEVA recover on the market at least for a short time or attract the attention of new investors.

The first of these is cooperation with the US defense industry. The company emphasizes that its technology meets the requirements for the Pentagon and that the company is actively involved in US defense sector decisions on advanced sensor systems. Defense is often distinguished by larger margins, longer contracts, and lower price pressure, so I believe any more serious order or partnership could significantly improve the results in the coming quarters. However, so far, it remains only a strategic direction without specific signed contracts or revenue from that segment.

Another possible catalyst is cooperation with the ZF Group, one of the world's largest car component manufacturers. AEVA hopes that its 4D LiDAR technology will become the cornerstone of other generations of autonomous driving systems. The company more recently announced a partnership with Daimler Truck, aiming to scale its 4D LiDAR into autonomous freight platforms. This sounds promising, but there is still no transparency on whether this partnership will result in actual mass production or signed orders. From what is known, it’s mostly a strategic agreement for now, which may or may not lead to commercial results anytime soon.

AEVA also highlights the technological flexibility of its LiDAR platforms. The company not only develops a car market, but also aims to adapt to industrial robots, unmanned aircraft, and smart cities. Theoretically, this increases the potential of the market, but in reality, these areas are further fragmented, with many competing technologies and different integration requirements. In addition, most of these segments are still developing regulatory systems, which means a long way to standardization and large-scale implementation.

From a short-term point of view, one of the main catalysts can become additional funding. If the company is able to successfully draw from its $125 million share purchase agreement without significant pressure on shares, it could stabilize liquidity and provide the ability to continue the development of operations without urgent cost reductions. However, it depends on market conditions and the appetite of investors, which is currently weak.

In conclusion, although AEVA has several potential growth catalysts, none of them have been clearly materialized. The value of the company on the market is still more based on technological promise than confirmed business performance, in my view.

Risks

There are inevitably many essential risks that affect both the short-term and long-term perspectives of the company. First of all, financial survival. AEVA is currently burning more than $10 million per month. At the end of Q2, the company had $49.8 million in cash and short-term investments. This could mean that without new capital raised, the company would last less than half a year. This circumstance might force the company to depend on additional funding, which directly poses a risk to current shareholders as the probability of dilution increases, as debt is not likely to be used in this case.

The company has a share sales agreement to raise up to $125 million. At first glance, it may seem like a financial safety net, but these funds would be raised through public share issues, and if it happens at the current (or lower) price, dilution of shareholders would be significant. This risk is particularly relevant to retail investors.

Another significant risk is the uncertainty around the market and the technology. AEVA is developing an advanced 4D LiDAR solution, but the autonomous driving sector still remains in the development phase. Despite high expectations over the past decade, most automakers have postponed autonomous systems implementation plans due to technical and regulatory challenges. For example, Tesla has gone away from LiDAR systems by choosing other methods (like cameras and AI combinations). This shows that LiDAR is not the only way to autonomous driving, and AEVA is currently building its business around this one direction, which might not be sustainable in my opinion.

Technological competition makes the situation even more difficult. Players like Luminar, Innoviz, or Hesai offer their solutions and have already established partnership models with car manufacturers. Although declaring cooperation with the ZF Group, AEVA has no known serial integration into mass production yet. In addition, the company does not have notable long-term contracts, which could mean that even after a record quarter, such as Q2, financial performance is unlikely to be sustainable. Small prototype orders cannot ensure business stability, in my view.

Finally, there is a risk of management. AEVA’s leadership continues to focus on the "vision", but specific financial results do not justify trust in the quick transition to a sustainable growth history. The company's strategy is still focused on a distant future, where LiDAR will become a market standard, but there is no guarantee at the moment that this will actually happen.

Valuation

Aeva Technologies is currently reaching the overvalued zone, especially if we compare it to other companies operating in the same sector. The main distinctive indicator is the Price/Sales (TTM), which is 101.35. This means that investors pay more than $100 for each $1 of revenue when the sector average varies by about 5 to 10. For example, EVLV has 9.26, NSSC 6.25, and OUST 9.33. This difference shows the strongly inflated expectations of the market.

The EV/Sales (TTM) ratio is also unfavorable—95.96. Compared to NSSC (5.63), LASR (4.55), or DAKT (0.92), AEVA looks extremely expensive even among other technology companies in the growth stage. Market valuation in this case does not reflect the existing volume or profitability of the business—a more speculative bet on commercial success in the distant future.

Price/Book reaches 15.52, which indicates that the market price is more than 15 times higher than the accounting value of the company. Other participants in the sector look much more moderate here: EVLV - 8.96, NSSC - 6.83, LASR - 4.80. I believe this does not have a clear fundamental basis and only reinforces the impression that the current price is not based on real financial results.

The AEVA looks significantly overvalued when looking at these main ratios. Such a situation can only survive with very strong technological breakthroughs or long-term investor faith.

Summary

AEVA Technologies currently remains one of the riskiest stories in the entire LiDAR sector, in my view. Despite the technological potential and several partnerships published, the company has no clear path to profitability, and the cash burn remains high. Even after a record Q2 2025, the company doesn't seem to be in a stable or strong position from a financial health perspective. Yes, the share price rose quite sharply if we look at YTD, but I simply do not see fundamental backing here.

The valuation remains high—around 100 times based on Price/Sales and EV/Sales. I think such numbers require a technological breakthrough in either rapid and exponential growth or great faith. None of these scenarios has yet been confirmed. In addition, the company has the opportunity to issue new shares in the near future for more than $100 million, which would increase the pressure on existing shareholders.

Despite several interesting technological elements - such as AEVA ATLAS or LG cooperation, the company's financial situation, slow commercial progress, and huge market optimism make this position extremely sensitive to correction. There are currently no clear catalysts that could justify the current valuation, in my opinion.

For my initial Seeking Alpha analysis of AEVA, I am rating the stock a SELL.
